transcript: Welcome to Lesson 6.3, Writing High-Converting Email Copy. In Lesson 6.2, you will learn the four campaign types and when each one belongs in your strategy. Now it is time to fill those campaigns with words that actually work. Because here is the truth about email marketing. The platform, the automation, and the design are all delivery infrastructure. The copy is the campaign. It is the words on the subject line that determine whether the email gets opened. It is the first sentence that determines whether it gets read. And it is the CTA that determines whether it drives any action at all. By the end of this lesson, you will have five subject line formulas. A complete email body framework. CTA copy rules. and a clear understanding of how tone and structure shift across all four campaign types. You will also receive the email copywriting template, a document you will use to draft every email before building it in GoHighLevel. Let's get into it. Let me show you the stakes before we get into the frameworks. Here are two emails sent to the same list on the same day at the same time with the same offer. The only variable is the copy. The week version has a subject line reading our monthly newsletter, October edition. The preview text says, click to read our latest updates. The body opens with, we are excited to share our latest offerings with you. The CTA button says, click here, this email achieved a 9% open rate, a 0.4% CTR, and zero conversions. The copy talks entirely about the sender. It has no hook, no relevant signal, and no reason to click. The strong version has a subject line reading, You are losing leads every day. Here is the fix. The preview text says, The 5-minute workflow that stops leads from going cold. The body opens with the reader's problem immediately. The CTA says, Show me the system. This email achieved a 41% open rate, a 9.2% CTR, and 14 conversions from the same list. Same infrastructure, same audience, same offer. The copy produced 4 times the opens and 23 times the clicks. This is why copy is the variable that changes everything in email marketing. Everything you learn in this lesson is aimed at consistently producing the result on the right side of that comparison. Your subject line is the most important piece of copy in your entire email. If it does not earn the open, nothing else gets a chance. Here are the five formulas that professional email copywriters use consistently. Formula number one is the curiosity gap. This formula hints at valuable information without fully revealing it. The reader cannot help but open because the answer is unresolved in their mind. An example, the reason your emails go to spam and how to fix it today. This formula works best for nurture campaigns where you want to pull warm leads into educational content. The critical rule, your email body must immediately deliver on the curiosity created. If you tease and then disappoint, your unsubscribe rate rises and your trust drops. Formula number two is the direct benefit. This formula states the specific outcome the reader will receive from opening. An example, get three more booked calls this week. Free template inside. This works best for promotional campaigns where the value of opening is the offer itself. Specificity is everything here. More leads is weak. Three more booked calls this week is compelling. Formula number three is the personal question. This formula asks a question the reader cannot help but answer in their own head. An example, are you still manually following up with every lead? If your persona is doing exactly this, they open immediately because the subject line feels like it was written specifically for them. This works best for welcome and nurture campaigns. The question must reflect your persona's actual lived experience, which is where the voice of customer research from Lesson 4.2 becomes directly applicable. Formula number four is the urgency or deadline. This formula creates time pressure around a specific action or opportunity. An example, closest tonight at midnight, last chance for the bonus. This formula belongs exclusively in promotional deadline emails, the final 24 to 48 hours of a campaign window. The non-negotiable rule, only use genuine deadlines. If your deadline resets or was never real your subscribers learn quickly that your urgency means nothing and your promotional emails stop working permanently Formula number five is the pattern interrupt This formula breaks inbox expectations with an unexpected format or statement. Examples, I made a mistake or this is embarrassing or bad news. These subject lines cut through a crowded inbox because they feel fundamentally different from marketing language. This formula works best for re-engagement campaigns where your goal is to get a cold contact to open an email they have been ignoring. The rule. The first line of the email must immediately honor the pattern interrupt. If the subject line says I made a mistake and the email body is a promotional pitch, the reader feels manipulated and unsubscribes. Always write three subject line variations before choosing one. Your best subject line is the one your specific persona cannot resist opening. Plan to test two versions using adubes split testing, a skill covered in lesson 6.9. Most email marketers write a strong subject line and then completely ignore preview text. This is a significant missed opportunity, and it is one you will not make. Preview text is the snippet of copy that appears beside or below the subject line in an inbox view before the email is opened. Most email clients display between 40 and 90 characters of preview text. Critically, preview text is read at the same time as the subject line. It is part of the open decision, not part of the email body. Together, the subject line and preview text or your two-sentence pitch for why this email is worth opening right now. There are four ways to use preview text strategically. The first is to extend the subject line, continue the thought it started, and add specificity. If the subject line says you are losing leads daily, the preview text says, here is the five-minute fix that runs automatically. The second is to add a second hook, introduce a new angle that complements the subject line. If the subject line offers a free template, the preview text adds, used by 847 specialists to automate follow-up in under an hour. The third is to reinforce urgency with a specific detail. If the subject line says closest tonight, the preview text says 11.59 p.m. Philippine Standard Time, six hours remaining. The fourth is to answer the subject line question. If the subject line asks why open rates are falling, the preview text hints at the answer and invites them to find out in two minutes. Three things you must never do with preview text. Never leave it blank. If you do, email platforms pull the first line of your email body, which is often technical filler like view this email in your browser. Never repeat the subject line word for word. It wastes the space entirely and adds no new information. And never use meaningless filler like click to read more. This signals that you have nothing compelling to say before they even open. Every high converting email, regardless of campaign type, length, or audience, follows the same four-part body structure. This is your master framework for every email you write from this point forward. Part number one is the hook. This is your first one to two sentences, and their only job is to stop the reader from closing the email within the first three seconds. Your hook must create curiosity, address a pain point directly, or make a bold statement that compels the reader to continue. Here is the most important single rule in email copywriting. Never open with, I hope this email finds you well, or any variation of it. It is the single most skipped opening line in email marketing because it signals immediately that nothing compelling follows. A strong hook instead says something like, you are probably losing 60% of your leads to a problem that takes 20 minutes to fix. That sentence creates a problem, implies a solution, and gives the reader a reason to keep reading. Part number two is the context. This is one to three sentences that show the reader you understand their specific situation. You name their problem, acknowledge the frustration, and signal that what follows is directly relevant to them. This is where voice of customer language belongs. The exact phrases your persona uses to describe their challenge. When your context section sounds like the reader's own inner monologue, they feel seen, and that feeling keeps them reading. Part number three is the value. This is the body of your email, the educational tip, the case study, the offer details, or the resource you promised. Every sentence in the value section must earn its place by advancing the reader toward the CTA. If a sentence does not do that, cut it. Enailed bodies for most campaign types should sit between 100 and 300 words Longer is not better Denser value in fewer words is always the goal Part number four is the CTA This is your final one to two sentences plus your button The sentence before the button bridges the value section to the action. It is not the call to action itself. It is the transition into it. Something like, if you are ready to stop losing leads to inconsistent follow-up, here is your next step. Then the button, show me the automation system. One action stated clearly with benefit language on the button. Here is the principle you commit to memory from this lesson. Hook, context, value CTA. Every email you write for the rest of your career follows this structure. The length changes, the tone changes, the campaign type changes, the structure does not. Now let me show you how the four-part framework adapts across the four campaign types from Lesson 6.2. Because while the structure is constant, the tone and emphasis shift significantly. For welcome campaigns, your hook is warm and grateful. Something like, you made a great decision downloading this guide. Your context orients the new subscriber and sets expectations for what is coming. Your value delivers the lead magnet plus one genuinely useful insight. Your CTA is low commitment. Download your guide, watch a short video. The tone throughout is welcoming and appreciative. Length is 150 to 250 words. For nurture campaigns, your hook opens with the reader's problem. Something like most businesses never fix this one thing about their follow-up. Your context acknowledges where they are in their journey. Your value is pure education, a tip, framework, case study, or curated resource. Your CTA drives a content click. Read the full case study, get the free template. The tone is expert but non-salesy. Length is 100 to 300 words. For promotional campaigns, your hook leads with urgency or a specific benefit. Your context reminds them of the journey that brought them here. They have been learning about this topic. Now they can implement it. Your value is the offer, what they get, what it costs, what changes for them. Your CTA drives a purchase or booking. The tone is confident and benefit-focused. Length is 200 to 400 words for the main offer email, significantly shorter for deadline emails. For re-engagement campaigns, your hook is a pattern interrupt. Your context acknowledges the silence directly and warmly. You notice they have not been opening and you want to know if they are still interested. Your value is your best content or an exclusive resource for returning contacts. Your CTA is a simple stay-or-go decision, Easy to click yes, easy to click no The tone is human and non-pressuring Length is under 150 words Brevity here signals respect for their time The CTA is the last thing your reader sees before making their decision 5 rules govern every email CTA you will ever write Rule number 1 1 CTA per email Multiple links competing for attention reduce total clicks across all of them. Decision paralysis is real. One email, one destination, one action. If you have two resources to share, send two emails. Rule number two. Describe the reward, not the action. Get the free workflow system describes what they receive. Click here describes what they do. Reward language wins every time because it frames the click as a gain rather than a task. Rule number three. Use a transition sentence before the button. The sentence immediately before your CTA button bridges the value section to the action. It is not the ask. It is the lead-in to the ask. If you are ready to automate your follow-up, here is your next step. Prepare the reader for the button that follows. Rule number four, use first-person language. Show me the system outperforms show you the system in split tests consistently. When the button text uses first-person language, the reader psychologically claims ownership of the result before clicking. The click becomes a confirmation of a decision already made. Rule number five, make it mobile tappable. Over 60% of emails are opened on mobile devices. A CTA button that is too small or too narrow to tap accurately on a phone is a CTA that does not get clicked. When you build emails in GoHighLevel in Lesson 6.5, you will verify every CTA button size in the mobile preview before sending. The test that governs all five rules The button text should complete the sentence I want to blank If the completed sentence sounds like a benefit it is strong If it sounds like a task or a chore rewrite it Before you write your next email, let me give you six mistakes to audit against because catching even two of these before sending will produce measurable improvement in your campaign performance. Mistake number one, opening with I hope this email finds you well or any similar pleasantry. It signals immediately that nothing compelling follows. Replace it with the reader's problem, a bold statement, or a curiosity-building question. Mistake number two, writing about yourself or your business instead of your reader. If your first paragraph contains more uses of we and I than you, rewrite it. The reader decides whether to keep reading based on whether they see themselves in the first few sentences. Mistake number three, including three or more CTAs in a single email. Every additional link you add to an email splits the reader's attention and reduces total clicks across all destinations. Commit to one action per email. Mistake number four, writing emails that are too long. Most readers scan before they read. An email that requires significant scrolling signals that you do not respect their time. Aim for 100 to 300 words for most campaigns. Cut every sentence that does not directly advance the reader toward the CTA. Mistake number five, using spam trigger language. Certain words and combinations, particularly phrases like free money, guaranteed, act now, no risk, and limited time offer, in close proximity, trigger spam filters before your email even reaches the inbox. Right naturally, authentic, conversational language outperforms promotional language for both deliverability and conversion. Mistake number six. Sending the same copy to your entire list regardless of where they are in their journey. A new lead who receives a hard promotional pitch before any trust has been built will unsubscribe. A warm, ready-to-buy lead who receives beginner educational content feels patronized. Segmentation is the solution, and it is covered in detail in Lesson 6.8. eliminating even two of these six mistakes from your next campaign will produce measurable results within a single cent let's lock in what you learned in this lesson the five subject line formulas for curiosity gap direct benefit personal question urgency or deadline and pattern interrupt each one maps to a specific campaign type in the reader state choose based on where your contact is in their journey, not based on what feels convenient. Preview text is a second subject line and part of the open decision. Never leave it blank, never repeat the subject line, and always use it to extend the hook or add a compelling second reason to open. The four-part email body framework is hook, context, value, and CTA. This structure applies to every email you will ever right, regardless of campaign type, length, or tone. The structure is the constant, everything else adapts. Copy tone and structure shift meaningfully across the four campaign types. Welcome is warm and expectation setting, nurture is educational and non-slipsy, promotional is confident and urgency-driven, and re-engagement is human, brief, and non-pressuring. Email CTAs follow five rules. One per email, reward language on the button, a transition sentence before the button, first-person phrasing, and mobile tapable sizing. The button text must complete I want to blank as a benefit, not a task. And the six most costly copy mistakes are weak openings, reader ignoring copy, multiple CTAs, excessive length, spam trigger language, and sending unsegmented copy to an entire list. Each one is avoidable with a simple pre-send audit. Coming up next is lesson 6.4, email design best practices. Now that you know what to write, lesson 6.4 teaches you how to present it visually. You will learn layout principles that make emails scannable and easy to act on, branding guidelines that build recognition across every send, and the mobile first design rules that govern how your emails look on the devices where most of your audience reads them. Great copy in a poorly designed email loses impact. Lesson 6.4 ensures your words land as powerfully as they read. Let's continue to Lesson 6.4.

visualDescriptions.description:
The image shows a screenshot of an online course slide titled "Writing High-Converting Email Copy." 

**Key Information:**
- **Title:** Writing High-Converting Email Copy
- **Subtitle:** Subject lines, Body structure, and CTAs — the complete copywriting framework for every email you will ever write
- **Section:** Module 4 - Lesson 4.3
- **Provider:** Libre

**What You'll Learn:**
- 6 subject line formulas that consistently drive opens across all campaign types
- How to use a proven text as a second subject line to multiply your open rate
- The 3-part anatomy framework that keeps readers engaged from that first word to CTA
- How copy tone and structure differ across Welcome, Newsletters, Promotional, and more
- Email CTA copy rules that turn readers into clickers
- The 6 most costly email copy mistakes and exactly how to fix them

**Visuals:**
- A graphic depicting an email with labeled sections: 
  - **H** Subject Line
  - **Body Copy** 
  - **CTA** 

**Additional Details:**
- Estimated Time: 16 Minutes

The image presents a concise summary of key takeaways from Lesson 6.3, titled "Key Takeaways" in bold green text. The title is accompanied by the subtitle "What you learned in this lesson." 

**Key Takeaways:**

*   **5-Subject Line Formulas:** 
    *   Curiosity Gap
    *   Direct Benefit
    *   Personal Question
    *   Urgency/Deadline
    *   Pattern Interrupt
*   **Preview Text Strategy:** 
    *   As a second subject line
    *   Never leave it blank
    *   Never repeat the subject line
    *   Always use it to extend the hook or add new points
*   **4-Part Body Framework:** 
    *   Hook
    *   Content
    *   Value
    *   CTA
*   **Tone Shifts by Campaign:** 
    *   Copy tone shifts meaningfully
    *   Welcome is warm
    *   Nurture is educational
    *   Promotional is urgent/benefit-focused
    *   Re-engagement is humanized
*   **CTA Roles:** 
    *   Follow 5 rules
    *   One CTA per mail
    *   Use relevant language on button
    *   Include transition sentence
    *   Use first-person phrasing
    *   Amuse mobile tappable size
*   **Copy Mistakes:** 
    *   Avoid weak openings
    *   Reader-ignoring copy
    *   Multiple CTAs
    *   Excessive length (>450 words)
    *   Spam trigger language
    *   And leading to unsubscribes

The image effectively summarizes the essential points from Lesson 6.3, providing a clear and organized overview of the key takeaways.

The image displays a simple graphic with the word "Libre" on it.

* The background of the image is green.
* In the center of the image, there is white text that reads "Libre". 
    + The font is bold and sans-serif.
    + The word is written in lowercase letters.

The image presents a concise guide to crafting effective email subject lines, titled "5 Subject Line Formulas That Consistently Drive Opens." The title is prominently displayed in black text at the top of the image, accompanied by a green line underneath. Below the title, a brief description explains that professional email copywriters utilize these frameworks for every campaign.

The main content of the image consists of five distinct subject line formulas, each represented by a colored bullet point and accompanied by a sample subject line. The formulas are:

* **THE CURIOSITY GAP**: A purple bullet point with the sample subject line "The reason your emails go to spam"
* **THE DIRECT BENEFIT**: A green bullet point with the sample subject line "Get 3x more booked calls this week!"
* **THE PERSONAL QUESTION**: A blue bullet point with the sample subject line "Are you still manually following up?"
* **URGENCY OR DEADLINE**: A red bullet point with the sample subject line "Close tonight at midnight"
* **THE PATTERN INTERRUPT**: A yellow bullet point with the sample subject line "Make $1 an acclaim"

Each formula is labeled with a best practice category, such as "BEST NURTURE" or "BEST PROMOTIONAL," indicating its suitability for specific types of emails.

At the bottom of the image, a green box provides additional tips for choosing the most effective subject line formula. The Libre logo is displayed in the top-right corner, adding a touch of branding to the design. Overall, the image offers a clear and concise guide to creating compelling email subject lines that drive opens.

The image presents a concise and informative guide to crafting effective email subject lines, titled "5 Subject Line Formulas That Consistently Drive Opens." The title is prominently displayed at the top, followed by a brief introduction that reads, "Professional email copywriters use these frameworks on every campaign — memorize them."

The main content of the image is organized into five sections, each representing a distinct formula for creating successful subject lines. These formulas are:

• **THE CURIOSITY GAP**: This formula involves creating curiosity without revealing too much information. An example of this type of subject line is "The reason your emails go to spam."
• **THE DIRECT BENEFIT**: This approach focuses on highlighting the benefits of opening the email. A sample subject line for this formula is "Get 3 more booked calls this week."
• **THE PERSONAL QUESTION**: This formula involves asking a personal question to pique the reader's interest. An example of this type of subject line is "Are you still manually following up?"
• **URGENCY OR DEADLINE**: This approach creates a sense of urgency or scarcity to encourage the reader to open the email. A sample subject line for this formula is "Close tonight at midnight."
• **THE PATTERN INTERRUPT**: This formula involves disrupting the reader's expectations with an unexpected subject line. An example of this type of subject line is "I made a mistake."

Each section includes a brief description and an example of a subject line that illustrates the formula. Additionally, the image features a green box at the bottom with text that provides tips for choosing a subject line, including testing variations before choosing one and using A/B split testing to optimize results.

Overall, the image provides a clear and concise guide to creating effective email subject lines using five proven formulas. By following these formulas and testing different approaches, email marketers can increase the likelihood of their emails being opened and read.

The image presents a slide from a presentation focused on effective email marketing strategies, specifically highlighting the importance of preview text in achieving a high open rate. The title, "Preview Text: Your Second Chance to Earn the Open," sets the tone for the content that follows.

**Key Points:**

* **Growth Daily**: 
	+ 3 ways to scale fast
	+ Read the latest growth hacks for your agency today
* **Your Name**: 
	+ You are being read daily
	+ Make sure to be the first to send (automate)
* **Sent Weekly**: 
	+ New Matter sent
* **What is it?**: 
	+ The first 50-60 characters that displays before or after the subject line
	+ It is read on the exact same screen as the subject line. Part of the email pre-header
* **Key Benefit**: 
	+ Open it
* **Extend the Subject Line**: 
	+ Continue the thought or add specificity to your subject
* **Add a Second Hook**: 
	+ Another angle to connect
* **Reinforce Urgency**: 
	+ Entice a response or get a reply with more details
* **Answer the Question**: 
	+ Expand on your subject with a benefit
* **What NOT To Do**: 
	+ Never leave it blank
	+ Never repeat content
	+ Never use filler ("Click to read more" or similar)

**Summary:**

The slide emphasizes the significance of crafting compelling preview text to increase email open rates. By following the provided strategies, such as extending the subject line, adding a second hook, reinforcing urgency, and answering questions, marketers can improve their chances of engaging their audience. Additionally, the slide highlights common mistakes to avoid, including leaving the preview text blank, repeating content, and using fillers. By mastering preview text, marketers can gain an immediate advantage in their email marketing efforts.

The image presents a slide titled "The 4-Part Email Body Framework" and features a green bar at the top with the word "Libre" in the upper right corner.

The framework consists of four key components:

**The Hook** (1 sentence): 
- Stop the reader in their tracks
- Create curiosity, address pain, or make a bold statement

**The Context** (1-2 sentences): 
- Show you understand the situation
- Bridge the gap to your solution using the customer's language

**The Value** (2-3 sentences): 
- Confirm the promised insight or offer
- Every sentence must make its place
- Examples: Educational, Story, Offer

**The CTA** (1 sentence): 
- Tell the reader exactly what to do next
- One clear action with clear language

A green button labeled "Sign up for the Automation Fixation" is located at the bottom of the slide.

The image presents a slide titled "The 4-Part Email Body Framework" in bold black text at the top left, with the word "Libre" in green text at the top right. 

* **Title and Subtitle**
	+ Title: "The 4-Part Email Body Framework"
	+ Subtitle: "Every high-converting email follows this structure — regardless of length or tone"

* **The 4-Part Email Body Framework**
	+ **The Hook**
		- 1-2 sentences
		- Job: Stop the reader in their tracks, Create curiosity, address pain, or make a bold statement
		- Example: "Statistically speaking 80% of the leads you produce that takes 20 minutes to fix..."
	+ **The Context**
		- 1-2 sentences
		- Job: Show you understand the situation. Bridge the past to your solution using Voice of Customer language
		- Example: "Your service business is experiencing..."
	+ **The Value**
		- 2-3 sentences
		- Job: Deliver the promised insight, or, offer. Every sentence must make its place
		- Examples: 
			- Educational
			- Story
			- Offer
	+ **The CTA**
		- 1 sentence
		- Job: Tell the reader exactly what to do. One clear action with simple language

* **Button**
	+ A green button with white text that reads "SIGN UP FOR THE AUTOMATION SYSTEM"

The framework provides a structured approach to writing effective email bodies, consisting of four key parts: The Hook, The Context, The Value, and The CTA. Each part has a specific job and characteristics, such as sentence count and language tone, to help create high-converting emails.

The image presents a table titled "How Copy Shifts Across 4 Campaign Types," which outlines the differences in tone, style, and approach across four distinct campaign types: Welcome, Nurture, Promotional, and Re-Engage.

**Campaign Types:**

*   **Welcome**
    *   **Tone Style:** Warm & Friendly
    *   **Content Approach:** Create a great 1st experience for new leads
    *   **Value Delivery:** Lead with a memorable story or animation snippet
    *   **CTA Type:** Low Commitment ("Download a free guide" or "Watch a video")
    *   **Ideal Length:** 100-250 words
    *   **Tone Summary:** Warm, welcoming, brief
*   **Nurture**
    *   **Tone Style:** Problem Aware
    *   **Content Approach:** Acknowledge their stage and current context
    *   **Value Delivery:** Educational content (tips, frameworks, case study)
    *   **CTA Type:** Content Click
    *   **Ideal Length:** 150-300 words
    *   **Tone Summary:** Expert, helpful, non-salesy
*   **Promotional**
    *   **Tone Style:** Urgency or Benefit
    *   **Content Approach:** Benefits or roadblocks to implement now
    *   **Value Delivery:** Offer details on what, why, and why now
    *   **CTA Type:** Purchase/ Funding ("Buy now" or "Sign up today")
    *   **Ideal Length:** 250-400 words
    *   **Tone Summary:** Confident, urgent, brief
*   **Re-Engage**
    *   **Tone Style:** Person to Person
    *   **Content Approach:** Acknowledge the silence or inactivity directly
    *   **Value Delivery:** Share your best content or exclusive offers
    *   **CTA Type:** Re-Engage or ("Re-Engage" or "Nice to hear from you")
    *   **Ideal Length:** Under 100 words
    *   **Tone Summary:** Human, direct, non-pressuring

The table provides a clear and concise comparison of the four campaign types, highlighting their unique characteristics and approaches. This information can be useful for marketers and businesses looking to tailor their content strategies to specific campaign goals and audience needs.

## **Email CTA Copy: The Rules That Drive Clicks**

The presentation slide focuses on best practices for writing effective **Call-to-Action (CTA) copy in emails**. Here are the key points:

### **1. One CTA per Email**  
 Include **only one CTA per email** to avoid overwhelming the reader and increase the likelihood of a click. Multiple CTAs can dilute the main action you want the reader to take.

### **2. Describe the Reward**  
 Clearly communicate the **benefit or reward** of clicking the CTA. Use action-oriented language that entices the reader by highlighting what they will gain.

### **3. Use Transition Sentences**  
 Use **transition sentences** to create a smooth flow between the email content and the CTA. This helps guide the reader logically toward the action you want them to take.

### **4. First-Person Language**  
 Write CTAs in **first-person language** (e.g., "Get *my* free worksheet template"). First-person CTAs tend to perform better as they create a sense of personal ownership.

### **5. Make it Mobile-Friendly**  
 Ensure that your CTA is **mobile-friendly**. Many users read emails on their phones, so the CTA button should be **large, clickable, and easy to tap**.

### **Weak vs. Strong CTAs**  
- **Weak CTAs** (shown in red text):  
  - Click here  
  - Learn more  
  - Sign up now  
  - Download  
- **Strong CTAs** (shown in green text):  
  - Get *my* free worksheet template  
  - Show me exactly how this works  
  - Reserve *my* spot in the program  
  - Send me the checklist now  

### **The "I Want To…" Test**  
A good CTA button should complete the sentence:  
**"The button text should compel the sentence: 'I want to [CTA].'"**  
If the sentence makes sense and feels natural, it is likely an effective CTA.

These guidelines help create clear, actionable, and persuasive CTAs that drive engagement and clicks in emails.

The image presents a concise guide to common email copy mistakes that can harm campaigns, titled "6 Email Copy Mistakes That Kill Campaigns." The title is displayed prominently at the top, with the word "Libre" in green text in the upper-right corner.

**Mistakes and Solutions**

The main content is organized into six sections, each highlighting a specific mistake and providing a solution:

*   **Opening with "I hope this email finds you well"**
    *   Why It’s Bad: It’s a lazy, overused opener that wastes space and doesn’t engage the reader.
    *   Instead: Open with the reader’s problem, a clear statement, or a story building.
*   **Writing about yourself/Business instead of your reader**
    *   Why It’s Bad: If the reader isn’t interested in you, why should they care about your business? They don’t.
    *   Instead: Make sure the subject line and the first paragraph make it about them.
*   **Including 2 or more CTAs in a single email**
    *   Why It’s Bad: Too many offers confuse and overwhelm your audience.
    *   Instead: Provide one clear call to action.
*   **Writing emails that are too long (>400 words)**
    *   Why It’s Bad: Most readers don’t have the attention span to read long emails.
    *   Instead: Aim for 100-200 words. Every sentence needs to have a purpose.
*   **Using spam trigger words (Free, Act Now, Guaranteed)**
    *   Why It’s Bad: Spam filters hate these words, and so do recipients.
    *   Instead: Write naturally. Artificial language usually projects promotional intent.
*   **Sending the same copy to your entire list**
    *   Why It’s Bad: It ignores audience segmentation and interests, which increases high unsubscribe rates.
    *   Instead: Segment by campaign type and target audience.

**Conclusion**

A dark gray bar at the bottom of the image features a blue circle with a white exclamation mark, accompanied by the text: "Eliminating even 2 of these 6 mistakes from your current campaigns will measurably improve opens, CTR, and conversions on the very next send." This infographic provides valuable insights for individuals looking to improve their email marketing strategies.
